does: DrugKLM is a hybrid framework that combines biomedical knowledge graphs with large language model-based mechanistic reasoning to identify and prioritize repurposable drugs. The system integrates structured biomedical relationships from knowledge graphs with LLM-based mechanistic inference to rank candidate therapeutics. Across multiple benchmark datasets, DrugKLM outperforms both knowledge graph-only and LLM-only baselines, including the prior method TxGNN. Critically, the framework's confidence scores correlate with functional molecular phenotypes: higher-scored candidates associate with transcriptional signatures linked to improved survival outcomes across 12 TCGA cancer cohorts. The approach systematically captures biologically perturbational signals rather than merely reflecting historical indication patterns, as validated through expert curation across five cancer types.
connects: This work bridges two distinct methodological traditions in computational drug discovery: structured knowledge graph approaches that leverage curated biomedical relationships, and more recent large language model methods that can reason over textual mechanistic information. Prior work in drug repurposing has relied either on graph-based similarity metrics or on text-mining approaches, but typically struggles to ground candidate selection in plausible biological mechanisms rather than connectivity patterns. The paper responds to limitations in methods like TxGNN, which excel at recall but lack mechanistic justification. It builds on broader trends integrating knowledge graphs with neural language models across biomedical AI, extending these hybrid architectures specifically to the drug repurposing problem where mechanistic interpretability is clinically essential.
enables: By demonstrating that mechanistic reasoning can be systematically integrated into therapeutic prioritization, this work opens pathways for more interpretable and clinically actionable drug discovery pipelines. The functional alignment between LLM-based confidence scores and molecular survival signatures suggests that such hybrid approaches could accelerate clinical validation workflows by pre-filtering candidates based on mechanistic coherence. Future work could extend this framework to other therapeutic domains beyond cancer, incorporate additional modalities such as protein structure or patient genomics, or develop more sophisticated mechanisms for reconciling conflicting signals from knowledge graphs and language models. The approach also enables better understanding of when mechanistic reasoning agreements outweigh historical patterns, potentially guiding the design of prospective computational screening studies for rare diseases where legacy indication data is sparse.
